title: Finra Fines, Suspends Texas Broker Over Unapproved Real Estate OBA
body_text:
The Financial Industry Regulatory Authority on Wednesday suspended for four months and fined $25,000 a former Wells Fargo broker over allegations that he participated in an unapproved real estate outside business activity.
George J. Cairnes allegedly from August 2015 to April 2023 “partnered with a firm customer to identify, buy, manage, and sell real estate” without firm permission, according to the settlement letter. The 23-year veteran broker created a limited liability company for the “partnership’s activities,” and he received compensation from the venture, the letter said.
Cairnes failed to provide prior notice to Wells about his real estate business and falsely attested on multiple firm compliance questionnaires that he was not participating in an OBA, according to the letter. Finra said he violated its Rule 3270, which prohibits brokers from receiving compensation from an OBA without approval, and its catch-all Rule 2010 requiring “high standards” of conduct.
Cairnes did not have legal representation in the settlement and did not respond to a request for comment.
A spokesperson for Wells declined to comment.
Wells in July 2023 terminated Cairnes over allegations that he “facilitated a loan between clients as well as loans and other transactions between a client and individuals associated with [Cairnes],” according to the letter. Finra initiated its investigation into Cairnes following Wells’ U5 termination notice.
In April 2024, Cairnes consented to a Disciplinary Order by the Texas State Securities Board, Finra wrote in the letter. According to the Texas Board, Cairnes was paid at least $175,000 for helping the client set up and run a real estate business, and he was barred from registering to be licensed in Texas for two years.
A Finra arbitration panel in August ordered Cairnes to pay Wells $180,000 over two
promissory notes
that he signed when he joined the firm in 2009 from the defunct Stanford Financial Group.
Cairnes also faces a pending customer dispute from April 2023 based on allegations that the client established a line of credit to loan money to Cairnes as well as his family members and friends which has not been fully repaid, according to his BrokerCheck. The disclosure does not specify the amount of money being sought.
Cairnes started his career at Merrill Lynch in 2000 and joined Stanford Financial in 2008, the year before it was seized by the federal government and its owner, Robert Allen Stanford, was charged with and later convicted of running an $8 billion
Ponzi scheme
.
After leaving Wells, Cairnes joined an independent broker-dealer, Chelsea Financial Services, but the firm withdrew his registration application in November 2023, according to the Texas State Securities Board order.

title: $6B Morgan Stanley Team Jumps to Wells Fargo Advisors in NYC
body_text:
A fast-growing Morgan Stanley team that managed $5.94 billion in assets jumped to Wells Fargo Advisors in New York City, according to a spokesperson for the hiring firm.
The Taylor Group at Morgan Stanley, which is led by 16-year broker C. James Taylor, produced $18.6 million in annual revenue, the Wells spokesperson said. The team reports to Michael Freiheit, branch manager at Wells’ Midtown Manhattan office in the GM building, and Patrick Baumann, New York City market leader.
“This is a highly driven team that operates with precision and purpose,” Baumann said in a statement. “They’re keen to take advantage of Wells Fargo’s full balance sheet, which gives them a clear runway to serve their clients holistically.”
The 19-person team includes advisors Shane Drumm, Michaella Irvine, Cameron Irvine, Marcus Briscoe, Jamison Embury, Roger McGlynn, Hunter Embury and Kyle Drumm as well as 10 support staff.
Their practice had ballooned in recent years as the group managed $1.2 billion in 2023, according to a Barron’s profile that year.
A source at a competing firm called the team “high quality” and said that their growth in assets was driven in part by their concentration of clients who are employees and executives at Nvidia. The chipmaker’s stock has surged more than 1,200% over the past five years.
Taylor first registered with Hennion & Walsh in 2009 and worked at Merrill Lynch for nine years before moving to Morgan Stanley in 2020, according to BrokerCheck. He did not respond to a request for comment.
A Wells spokesperson also did not return a request for comment regarding the team’s client base.
A Morgan Stanley spokesperson did not immediately provide a comment. The firm has been leaning into its workplace business, including servicing the corporate stock plans for Fortune 500 companies, as a way to funnel clients to its advisors.
Meanwhile, Wells has been aggressively pricing deals for large, high-growth teams, recruiters have said. Its offers for a group like Taylor’s could include 275% of trailing-12 revenue in upfront cash, which would put it at or near the top of the market not including back-end bonuses, the source said.
Wells Fargo executives earlier this month attributed the bank’s recruiting success to its multi-channel approach, including its FiNet independent brokerage and RIA custody unit launching later this year. Roger Gershman, an industry recruiter who was not involved in the move but was familiar with the team, claimed they narrowed their search to three firms but wanted a place that would provide a long-term path to independence.
“We’ve talked a lot about the momentum that we’re building there,” Chief Financial Officer Mike Santomassimo said earlier in April about the wealth division, which is led by Barry Sommers . “That business just grows through improving just naturally the net flows we’re getting [and] the recruiting we’re seeing.”
The wirehouse earlier this week reeled in a UBS Wealth Management USA team managing $466 million in Florida. In April, a UBS team with $2.1 billion joined Wells’ independent Financial Network.
–Mason Braswell contributed to this story.
Great  and growing platform and offering for clients.  Big payday for the team.  And then independence. Nice.
Your email address will not be published. Required fields are marked *
Team produced $10 million in annual revenue, according to a source familiar with the move.
